skill.name: event-driven-architecture
skill.description: Reusable event-driven architecture patterns for Kafka, Redis, Dapr, event sourcing, CQRS, sagas, and Kubernetes. Helps teams design resilient asynchronous microservices.
skill.summary: Design event-driven microservices with messaging, event storage, sagas, CQRS, Dapr, and deployment patterns.
skill.author: Azeem-2

security_audit.summary: Static analysis raised many alerts, but most are false positives from Markdown code fences, identifier names, message keys, and descriptive text. I found no evidence of shell execution, prompt injection, weak cryptography, or credential exfiltration. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because Dapr examples perform network calls and include secret-store access patterns.

medium_findings.title: Network-Capable Dapr Client Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill includes sample Dapr HTTP client code that can invoke services, bindings, and local Dapr APIs. This is expected for the skill domain, but generated implementations should keep endpoints local or explicitly trusted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The HTTP client and request calls are explicit and semantically tied to Dapr integration. I did not find evidence of malicious exfiltration domains, so the risk is operational rather than hostile.

medium_findings.title: Secret Store Access Pattern Requires Least Privilege
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The Dapr helper can retrieve secrets by key, and the Kubernetes example enables the Dapr secrets API and Kubernetes secret store. This is legitimate infrastructure code, but broad permissions could expose application secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The secret retrieval endpoint and Kubernetes secret references are directly visible. The pattern is common for Dapr deployments, but it needs scoped permissions and review before production use.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The external command alerts point to Markdown code fence delimiters around Python and YAML examples. I found no subprocess, os.system, eval, exec, curl, or wget usage in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown delimiters or prose after code blocks, not executable shell syntax. Targeted search found no actual command execution APIs.

low_findings.title: Connection String Alerts Are Parameterized Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The database connection string alerts map to Redis constructor parameters and kwargs, not hardcoded credentials or environment variable reads. No evidence found of credential harvesting or exfiltration at those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code stores a caller-provided Redis URL and passes it to aioredis. This may contain deployment credentials in real use, but the skill does not read or leak them.

low_findings.title: Key File Alerts Are Message Key Fields
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The sensitive file alerts are triggered by Kafka and Redis message key fields. These are routing identifiers, not certificate files, private keys, or filesystem paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each cited location uses a message key field inside broker examples. No certificate material or key file path is present at these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography alerts match words such as description, source, aggregate type, and deserializer. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or custom encryption logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited terms are schema descriptions, event fields, and JSON deserializers. Targeted search did not reveal weak cryptographic algorithms.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Identifier and Query Usage
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reconnaissance alerts are ordinary event IDs, stream IDs, tenant IDs, and SQL query examples. No evidence found of host enumeration, filesystem discovery, or environment reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are database and in-memory event-store examples. They do not inspect the host system or collect runtime environment details.

low_findings.title: SQLite File Alerts Are PostgreSQL Pool Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The SQLite database file alerts map to PostgreSQL event-store examples using a database pool. No evidence found of local database file access or filesystem persistence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines reference a PostgreSQL connection pool and SQL execution. They do not open SQLite files or interact with arbitrary filesystem paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP Requests to Dapr Runtime
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples create an httpx client and call Dapr service invocation, bindings, and secrets endpoints. This requires endpoint trust boundaries and input validation in derived implementations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network pattern is explicit and intentional for Dapr. The risk depends on how users bind hosts, methods, metadata, and permissions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dapr Secrets API Enabled in Deployment Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The Kubernetes example allows the Dapr secrets API and configures a Kubernetes secret store. Implementations should restrict secret access and avoid exposing Dapr APIs beyond trusted services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The YAML explicitly enables secrets and references Kubernetes secrets. This is a valid deployment pattern with permission risk if copied without hardening.

content.value_statement: Designing reliable event-driven systems is complex because messaging, state, retries, and deployment choices interact. This skill provides reusable patterns for Kafka, Redis, Dapr, event sourcing, CQRS, sagas, and Kubernetes deployment.
actual_capabilities: Defines base event schemas with versioning, correlation IDs, tenant context, priorities, retries, and delayed processing.
Shows in-memory and PostgreSQL event store patterns with stream queries, type queries, and aggregate snapshots.
Provides Kafka and Redis broker abstractions for publish, subscribe, batch publish, and topic creation.
Demonstrates saga and CQRS patterns for commands, projections, compensation, and read models.
Includes Dapr patterns for state, pub/sub, service invocation, bindings, and secret access.
Provides Kubernetes Dapr deployment examples for pubsub, state store, secret store, metrics, and tracing.
limitations: Examples are reference patterns and need adaptation for each application domain and runtime.
It does not provision Kafka, Redis, PostgreSQL, Dapr, Kubernetes, or cloud infrastructure.
Security settings are illustrative, so authentication, authorization, and secret policies need review.
Python snippets assume async dependencies that are not bundled with the skill.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Event Platform
use_cases.description: Define core events, broker choices, storage patterns, and deployment components before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Implement Reliable Microservice Workflows
use_cases.description: Use saga, retry, compensation, and CQRS patterns to coordinate asynchronous service behavior.

use_cases.title: Prepare Dapr-Based Deployments
use_cases.description: Translate application needs into Dapr pubsub, state store, secret store, metrics, and tracing examples.

prompt_templates.title: Explain an Event-Driven Design
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner orientation for a new system design.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the event-driven architecture skill to explain the main components for a simple order workflow. Include events, broker, storage, and consumers.

prompt_templates.title: Create Event Contracts
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate planning for a domain model.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design event contracts for this domain: [describe domain]. Include event names, required metadata, versioning, correlation IDs, and consumer responsibilities.

prompt_templates.title: Design Saga and CQRS Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a saga and CQRS read model for [workflow]. Include commands, events, compensation steps, projection updates, retries, and failure handling.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this event-driven design for production readiness: [paste design]. Check broker configuration, idempotency, observability, secret access, Dapr permissions, and replay strategy.

output_examples.input: A team asks for a task management event model.
output_examples.output: The response outlines task.created and task.updated events, required metadata, consumer responsibilities, projection updates, and retry behavior.

output_examples.input: A platform engineer asks how to deploy Dapr components for event processing.
output_examples.output: The response describes pubsub, state store, secret store, tracing, and metrics components with security notes for production review.

output_examples.input: A backend engineer asks for a saga design for payment processing.
output_examples.output: The response gives ordered saga steps, compensation actions, timeout handling, idempotency checks, and event publication boundaries.

best_practices: Define event contracts, versions, correlation IDs, and ownership before writing consumers.
Scope broker, database, Dapr, and secret-store permissions to the minimum required access.
Test idempotency, replay, compensation, and observability before production rollout.
anti_patterns: Publishing unversioned events with inconsistent payloads across services.
Letting consumers depend on private producer database schemas instead of events.
Granting broad Dapr, broker, or secret-store access to every service.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete application?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture patterns and example snippets that must be adapted to a real application.

faq.question: Which messaging systems does it cover?
faq.answer: It includes Kafka and Redis examples, plus Dapr pubsub patterns that can map to several brokers.

faq.question: Does it include event sourcing and CQRS?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes event store, snapshot, command, event, projection, and read model patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with Dapr deployments?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Dapr client patterns and Kubernetes component examples for pubsub, state, secrets, metrics, and tracing.

faq.question: Is the generated code production ready?
faq.answer: No. Users must add authentication, authorization, validation, monitoring, tests, and environment-specific hardening.

faq.question: Why does the audit show medium risk?
faq.answer: The examples include network calls and secret-store access. These are legitimate but need careful permissions and endpoint controls.
